text.title: competitor-analysis Core Rules
text.summary: Migrated testable rules for competitor-analysis. Verify and refine before relying on for production.

rule.statement: Identify all three competitor types: direct (same product, same audience), indirect (different product, same problem), and future (emerging or could pivot). Target 5-10 direct, 3-5 indirect, 2-3 future.
rule.rationale: Analysing only direct competitors misses the substitutes customers actually use and the entrants who will arrive in 18 months.

rule.statement: Plot each competitor on a 2-axis matrix (price vs feature breadth) and identify empty quadrants or underserved positions before selecting your own positioning.
rule.rationale: Empty quadrants reveal market gaps; overcrowded quadrants signal where differentiation is hardest. Visual mapping surfaces the whitespace that a feature list alone obscures.

rule.statement: Analyse each competitor across all 8 dimensions. Do not skip dimensions—missing ones leave blind spots that surface in the gap analysis.
rule.rationale: A partial analysis produces a partial gap table. Investors and customers will probe the dimensions you skipped.

rule.statement: Differentiate on a competitor weakness, not on feature parity. Scrape 1-star reviews and convert the top 3 complaints into your positioning message.
rule.rationale: Copying competitors produces table-stakes features. Converting their biggest complaints into your headline creates a concrete, testable claim that resonates with dissatisfied users already searching for alternatives.

rule.statement: Write a positioning statement in the canonical template form before finalizing the gap analysis.
rule.rationale: Forces the team to articulate the differentiation concretely and reveals whether the gap identified is actually usable as messaging.

rule.statement: Pin target geography in every prompt and reject any competitor whose primary market differs from your target region.
rule.rationale: Without geography pinning, agents return competitors ranked by Google's global SERP, which skews toward US companies even when the user's market is EU or APAC.

rule.statement: Run per-competitor snapshot tasks in parallel, synthesis serially. Cuts wall-clock by 5-8x and isolates per-competitor context windows.
rule.rationale: Each competitor's pricing page, review scrape, and feature list is independent. Parallelising collection then serialising synthesis is the only pattern that scales beyond 5 competitors without context degradation.

rule.statement: Seed the competitor list with humans + customer interviews before agents—agents over-index on Google's first page and miss "what my friend uses".
rule.rationale: Algorithmic discovery is fast but biased. Human seed lists capture the substitutes customers actually use.

rule.statement: Force the agent to cite a URL for every factual cell (price, founding year, employee count); reject any cell without a source.
rule.rationale: Sourced claims are auditable. Unsourced cells are liabilities in investor meetings and marketing copy.

rule.statement: Run snapshot agents in parallel (one per competitor), synthesis serially. Cuts wall-clock by 5-8x and isolates per-competitor context.
rule.rationale: Each competitor's pricing page, review scrape, and feature list is independent. Parallelising collection then serialising synthesis is the only pattern that scales beyond 5 competitors without context degradation.

rule.statement: Always include "future" competitors (AI startups, big-tech adjacencies)—they are the real threat in 18 months.
rule.rationale: Current market leaders do not predict category shifts. Future competitors require a concrete signal (job posting, acquisition, beta product) but must not be omitted.

rule.statement: Keep the matrix to ≤8 dimensions; more columns degrade the gap analysis signal.
rule.rationale: Excessive dimensions dilute focus and make visual patterns harder to spot.

rule.statement: Re-run quarterly with the same template; diff the markdown to see which competitors moved.
rule.rationale: Quarterly re-runs surface pricing escalations, feature launches, and team growth. A diff between two snapshots is more actionable than a one-time matrix.

rule.statement: Store the seed competitor list in version control (.aidocs/product_docs/_competitors.yml) so re-runs are reproducible.
rule.rationale: Reproducibility enables credible quarterly diffs and prevents drift in competitor selection.

rule.statement: Mark every estimated number with ~ or (est.) and never let the agent strip those hedges in the synthesis pass.
rule.rationale: Unhedged estimates in a published matrix damage credibility. Investors and customers expect transparency about data quality.
